text: Our patient is a twenty-four year old male with refractory ulcerative colitis on the background of a cadaveric kidney transplant. Despite maximal immune suppression for his disease as well as his transplant, his symptoms of urgency, frequency and pain continued to be severe so he proceeded for total colectomy with end ileostomy. Here we can see a well healed scar in the patient's right iliac fossa from the kidney transplantation. A single port laparoscopic method was used with this location marked inferior and to the right of the umbilicus. This will be the only site of operative access for his operation and also represents the ultimate site for his end ileostomy.

The extraperitoneal transplanted kidney is visible in the right iliac fossa, with the transplanted ureter traveling down into the pelvis. The appendix is identified, and the small intestine is removed from the pelvis by positioning the patient head down and left side up, providing exposure of the distal sigmoid colon and proximal rectum. The dissection begins distally at the rectal sigmoid, proceeding proximally in a close pericolic plane after distal sigmoid staple transection. The transplanted ureter and renal artery are clearly visible traveling into the pelvis.

The surgeon begins the dissection of the distal sigmoid colon from its mesocolon using a Medtronic ligature device for hemostatic control and precise dissection. A window is created through the mesocolon for stapled distal transection using an ENDO-GIA-SIXTY device. The fully dissected distal colon is visible, and the stapler is placed into a separate finger of the glove port for transection, ensuring no pelvic side wall structures are caught within the stapler fire.

The surgeon detaches the sigmoid colon from the rectum and begins dissecting the sigmoid mesocolon from the sigmoid colon, extending proximally. The assisting instrument provides traction while the ligature device is operated by the first surgeon. The sigmoid colon is moved medially, revealing the left ureter along the pelvic wall. The dissection remains in a close pericolic plane to avoid the retroperitoneal ureter and preserve the gonadal vessels. A bleeding mesocolic vessel is coagulated using the ligature device.

The surgeon approaches the descending colon, dissecting its lateral attachments using a lateral approach. The distracting instrument is frequently reapplied to maintain appropriate tension on the tissue. The patient is tilted into a head up, left side up position to facilitate bringing the splenic flexure towards the operation site. Both medial and lateral dissection techniques are employed, and the omentum is removed from its anterior and posterior attachments to the transverse colon.

The surgeon mobilizes the transverse colon by dissecting it from the omentum and its mesocolon, entering the lesser sac while carefully protecting the stomach and its vessels. The middle colic arteries are visible in this quadrant, and the surgeon ensures they are securely sealed.

The surgeon mobilizes the transverse colon along its distal two-thirds and proceeds to dissect the cecum distally along the ascending colon. The cecum, ileum, and appendix are elevated to facilitate dissection of the associated mesentery, revealing a clear plane between the right colon and the transplanted kidney. The cecum and terminal ileum are freed, and dissection continues cephaladilaterally towards the hepatic flexure in a stepwise manner. The lateral attachments are completely dissected, and the surgeon moves medially to dissect the mesocolon of the right colon, ensuring the duodenum is visible and preserved.

The surgeon removes the distal end of the colon through the single port access site after complete mobilization. An extracorporeal separation of the ileum from the cecum is performed, and the ileum is returned to the abdominal cavity. Relaparoscopy is conducted to ensure hemostasis, wash out any pooled fluid or blood, and verify the orientation of the distal ileum for end ileostomy formation.
